claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children, and what are their names and ages?

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex, who is 16, and Blake, who is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (16) and Blake (5). Let me ask you some questions to assess eligibility for each of them.

Let me start with some basic questions:

1. **Do you live in the UK?** (or are you a Crown servant or member of the armed forces posted overseas?)

2. **Are both Alex and Blake living with you?**

These are important starting points for determining Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

As for the children, Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake (5) lives with you, but Alex (16) does not.

For **Alex (16)** who doesn't live with you, I need to understand more about their situation:

**Where does Alex live?** For example:
- With another parent or family member?
- In hospital or residential care?
- At boarding school?
- Somewhere else?

This will help me determine if you might still be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex even though they don't live with you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent - my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So Alex lives with your ex-partner.

This is important because **generally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child** - and it's usually the person the child lives with who has priority.

Let me ask a few more questions:

1. **Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

2. **For Alex specifically** - you mentioned they are 16. Is Alex currently in approved education or training? (This is important because Child Benefit rules change when a child turns 16 - they generally need to be in approved education or training for you to continue receiving Child Benefit until they turn 20.)

3. **For Blake (5)** who lives with you - is Blake in hospital, in local authority care, or living at home with you normally?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
1. No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I contribute towards Alex's upkeep - I help with things like school costs and other expenses.

2. Yes, Alex is still in education - doing A-levels at sixth form college.

3. Blake lives at home with me normally - not in hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that detail. Let me clarify a few more things:

**For Alex:**
Even though you contribute to Alex's upkeep and they're in education, the key question is about where Alex lives. Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, **your ex-partner would generally have priority to claim Child Benefit for Alex** because they're the person Alex lives with. 

However, I need to ask: **Does your ex-partner want to claim Child Benefit for Alex, or have they chosen not to?** If they're not claiming and don't want to claim, there may be circumstances where you could potentially claim.

**For Blake:**
Since Blake is 5 years old and lives with you normally, this looks more straightforward. Let me ask:

1. **Are you responsible for Blake?** (meaning you're the parent or someone who has taken on responsibility for them)

2. **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
**For Alex:**
No, my ex-partner doesn't want to claim Child Benefit for Alex. That's why I'm looking into claiming it myself, since I'm contributing to their costs.

**For Blake:**
Yes, I'm Blake's parent, so I'm responsible for them. But actually, Blake's other parent does claim Child Benefit for Blake already - they live with us and they're the one who claims it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying both situations. This is very helpful.

Let me make sure I understand correctly:

**For Blake:** Blake's other parent lives with you and Blake in the same household, and **they are already claiming Child Benefit for Blake**. Is that correct?

If yes, then you wouldn't be able to claim for Blake as well, because **only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child** - even if you both live together and are both responsible for Blake.

**For Alex:** Your ex-partner doesn't want to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Since Alex is in approved education (A-levels) and under 20, there may be potential for you to claim. However, I need to clarify:

**When you say you "contribute towards Alex's upkeep" - do you contribute at least the same amount as anyone else who contributes to Alex's costs?** This is relevant because if someone else contributes more than you do, they would have priority to claim.

Also, **does Alex live with your ex-partner full-time, or does Alex spend any time living with you** (for example, staying with you on weekends or during holidays)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
**For Blake:** 
Yes, that's correct - Blake's other parent lives with us in the same household and they claim the Child Benefit for Blake.

**For Alex:**
I contribute regularly to Alex's costs - I send money each week for things they need. I'd say I contribute more than anyone else towards their upkeep, really. 

Alex lives with my ex-partner full-time - they don't come to stay with me on weekends or anything like that. They're settled there with school and everything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that detail. Let me make sure I have the complete picture:

**For Blake (5):**
- Lives with you
- You're the parent
- Blake's other parent (who also lives with you) is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- **Result: You cannot claim for Blake** because only one person can receive Child Benefit per child, and Blake's other parent is already claiming it.

**For Alex (16):**
- Lives full-time with your ex-partner (not with you)
- Your ex-partner doesn't want to claim Child Benefit
- You contribute regularly (more than anyone else) to Alex's upkeep
- Alex is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form)

This is a specific situation. According to the rules, **the person the child lives with has priority to claim Child Benefit**. Even though your ex-partner doesn't want to claim, and even though you contribute to Alex's costs, the fact that Alex lives with your ex-partner full-time (and not with you) means **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

The general rule is that Child Benefit goes to the person the child lives with. Contributing financially to a child who lives elsewhere doesn't establish eligibility if the child doesn't live with you.

Let me provide you with my final assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**:

**For Alex (16):**
- Although Alex is in approved education and you contribute financially to their upkeep, Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner
- Under Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority to claim
- Even though your ex-partner doesn't want to claim, you cannot claim because Alex doesn't live with you

**For Blake (5):**
- Although Blake lives with you and you're their parent, Blake's other parent (who lives in the same household) is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child
- Since Blake's other parent is already claiming, you cannot also claim

I understand this may not be the answer you were hoping for, but these are the rules as set out in the UK Child Benefit system. If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if Alex comes to live with you, or if Blake's other parent stops claiming), your eligibility could be different.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. However, Alex lives full-time with their other parent (the claimant's ex-partner). Under UK Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority to claim Child Benefit. Even though the claimant contributes financially to Alex's upkeep and the ex-partner doesn't want to claim, the claimant is not eligible because Alex does not live with them. Only the person the child lives with (or in very limited circumstances, someone the child lives with for part of the time) can claim Child Benefit.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with the claimant, who is Blake's parent. However, Blake's other parent, who also lives in the same household with both the claimant and Blake, is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (16) and Blake (5). For Alex, although the child is in approved education and the claimant contributes financially, Alex lives full-time with the other parent, which means the claimant is not eligible - the person the child lives with has priority. For Blake, although the child lives with the claimant, Blake's other parent (who lives in the same household) is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim per child. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. However, Alex lives full-time with their other parent (the claimant's ex-partner). Under UK Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority to claim Child Benefit. Even though the claimant contributes financially to Alex's upkeep and the ex-partner doesn't want to claim, the claimant is not eligible because Alex does not live with them. Only the person the child lives with (or in very limited circumstances, someone the child lives with for part of the time) can claim Child Benefit.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with the claimant, who is Blake's parent. However, Blake's other parent, who also lives in the same household with both the claimant and Blake, is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (16) and Blake (5). For Alex, although the child is in approved education and the claimant contributes financially, Alex lives full-time with the other parent, which means the claimant is not eligible - the person the child lives with has priority. For Blake, although the child lives with the claimant, Blake's other parent (who lives in the same household) is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim per child. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.
